section.title: When Israel Went Out from Egypt
section.sectionRationale: The second psalm of the Egyptian Hallel, this compact hymn compresses the exodus and wilderness years into vivid poetic snapshots, personifying sea, river, mountains, and hills as trembling before the presence of the LORD who once split the waters and later split the rock.
section.thesis: The same LORD who made the sea flee and the Jordan turn back at Israel's exodus is the LORD before whom the whole earth must tremble now, the God who turns hard rock into flowing water for His thirsty people.
section.frameNote: Two matched movements: the exodus and creation's tiptoeing response, verses 1-4, and the psalmist's probing questions answered by a call for all the earth to tremble before the God who provides water from the rock, verses 5-8.

units.label: Judah became God's sanctuary
units.note: the exodus and creation on tiptoe

verses.text: When Israel departed from Egypt, the house of Jacob from a people of foreign tongue,
Judah became God's {{miqdash|sanctuary}}, Israel His dominion.
The sea observed and fled; the Jordan turned back;
the mountains skipped like rams, the hills like lambs.

units.label: Tremble, O earth, at the presence of the Lord
units.note: probing questions, water from the rock

verses.text: Why was it, O sea, that you fled, O Jordan, that you turned back,
O mountains, that you skipped like rams, O hills, like lambs?
Tremble, O earth, at the presence of the Lord, at the presence of the God of Jacob,
who turned the rock into a pool, the {{challamish|flint}} into a fountain of water!

miqdash.gloss: Sanctuary, holy place. That Judah itself becomes God's dwelling place shows the exodus was never merely political rescue but the creation of a people among whom God would personally live.

challamish.gloss: Flint, a notably hard stone. That God turns the hardest, most unyielding rock into a fountain underscores that no obstacle, however impenetrable, resists His power to provide for His thirsty people.

basic.context: This brief hymn compresses roughly forty years of Israel's history, the crossing of the Red Sea, the giving of the law at Sinai, and the provision of water in the wilderness, into eight vivid lines, personifying sea, river, and mountains as fleeing and dancing before the presence of the LORD.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm's poetic device, addressing nature directly and asking why it trembled, is not mere literary flourish but a summons to the reader to recognize that the same overwhelming presence that once moved creation itself is present and active now, still worthy of trembling reverence.
basic.originalLanguages: Judah becomes God's miqdash, sanctuary, verse 2, the dwelling place of His presence among His people. And in the psalm's climactic image, God turns challamish, flint, an especially hard and unyielding stone, into a fountain of water, showing that no obstacle can resist His provision.
basic.typology: The rock that gives water in the wilderness, recalled in verse 8, is identified by Paul as a type of Christ, for they drank from the spiritual Rock that followed them, and that Rock was Christ, 1 Corinthians 10:4, the true and better source of living water for God's thirsty people.
basic.christ: Paul explicitly identifies the rock that gave Israel water in the wilderness as a type of Christ, 1 Corinthians 10:4, so the flint turned to a fountain in this psalm anticipates Jesus, who offers living water welling up to eternal life, John 4:10-14, and who cried out at the feast, If anyone is thirsty, let him come to Me and drink, John 7:37-39. Luke describes Jesus' own coming departure as an exodus, Luke 9:31, linking His death and resurrection directly to the deliverance this psalm celebrates.
basic.god: God's mere presence is so overwhelming that seas flee and mountains leap like startled lambs; He personally makes His dwelling among His redeemed people and provides abundantly for them even from the hardest, most unpromising sources.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 14:21-22 records the parting of the Red Sea recalled in verse 3. Joshua 3:14-17 records the Jordan's parting for the second, later generation. 1 Corinthians 10:4 identifies the rock of verse 8 as a type of Christ, the true source of living water.
basic.oneStory: The LORD whose presence made the sea flee and the rock pour water at the exodus is the same LORD whose presence the whole earth must tremble before, and the rock that gave Israel water in the wilderness points forward to Christ, the true Rock, who gives living water to everyone who is thirsty and leads His people through a greater exodus, from death into life.

context.literary: Two symmetrical stanzas of four verses each: the first records the exodus and creation's response, verses 1-4; the second turns to interrogate that same creation before calling all the earth to tremble at the LORD's presence, verses 5-8, with vivid parallelism and personification driving every line.
context.historical: The psalm recalls the exodus from Egypt, Exodus 12-14, the wilderness provision of water from the rock, Exodus 17:6 and Numbers 20:11, and Israel's second river crossing at the Jordan under Joshua, Joshua 3:13-17, compressing decades of salvation history into a single compact hymn.
context.canonical: As the second psalm of the Egyptian Hallel, Psalms 113-118, this psalm is recited at Passover immediately after Psalm 113, retelling the exodus story that the feast itself commemorates, and it was almost certainly among the hymns Jesus and His disciples sang at the Last Supper, Matthew 26:30.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To retell the exodus and wilderness story in a way that moves worshipers from historical memory to present reverence, insisting that the God who once made creation itself tremble and dance is the same God who must be feared and trusted now.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes specific historical acts of deliverance and provision; it prescribes the abiding posture of trembling reverence before God's presence and confident trust that no obstacle, however hard or impenetrable, can resist His power to provide.

debates.question: Is the personification of the sea, Jordan, mountains, and hills merely poetic imagery, or does it carry a stronger theological claim?

views.label: The language is vivid Hebrew poetic personification, a literary device to dramatize God's power over creation
views.case: Hebrew poetry regularly personifies natural elements, as in Psalm 29:3-9 and Psalm 97:4-5, using this device to communicate real historical events, the Red Sea and Jordan crossings, in heightened poetic form rather than claiming creation possesses independent consciousness.

views.label: The imagery reflects a genuine biblical conviction that all creation is responsive to its Creator's voice and presence
views.case: Scripture elsewhere describes creation groaning, Romans 8:22, and Jesus rebuking wind and waves that obey Him, Mark 4:41, suggesting the psalm's personification points to a real, though mysterious, creaturely responsiveness to God rather than mere literary decoration.

debates.bearing: Either reading affirms the same central truth: God's presence and word hold absolute authority over the created order, whether expressed through vivid literary device or a deeper creaturely responsiveness, and this authority calls for human trembling and trust as well.

study.originalLanguages: Judah itself becomes God's miqdash, sanctuary, verse 2, the dwelling place of His presence among His people. And in the psalm's climactic image, God turns challamish, flint, an unyielding and especially hard stone, into a fountain of water, verse 8, showing that no obstacle, however impenetrable, resists His provision.

typology.type: The rock that gave water in the wilderness, verse 8
typology.fulfillment: Identified by Paul as a type of Christ, for they drank from the spiritual Rock that followed them, and that Rock was Christ, 1 Corinthians 10:4.
Luke describes Jesus' own coming death and resurrection in Jerusalem as an exodus, Luke 9:31, the pattern of redemption this psalm celebrates brought to its fullest and final form.

study.christ: Paul identifies the rock that gave Israel water in the wilderness, recalled in verse 8, as a type of Christ Himself, 1 Corinthians 10:4, so this psalm's picture of flint becoming a fountain anticipates Jesus, who offers living water welling up to eternal life, John 4:10-14, and who cried out, If anyone is thirsty, let him come to Me and drink, John 7:37-39. Luke's Gospel describes Christ's own coming death and resurrection as an exodus, Luke 9:31, showing that the deliverance this psalm celebrates finds its ultimate and greater fulfillment in the cross.
study.god: God's mere presence is so overwhelming that seas flee and mountains leap like startled lambs before it. He makes His personal dwelling among His redeemed people and provides abundantly for them even out of the hardest, most unpromising sources, flint turned to flowing water.

crossReferences.note: Records the parting of the Red Sea that verse 3 recalls.
Records the Jordan River's parting for the generation entering the promised land, echoed alongside the Red Sea in verse 3.
Paul identifies the rock providing water in the wilderness as a type of Christ, the true source of living water.

study.oneStory: The LORD whose presence made the sea flee and turned flint into a fountain at Israel's exodus is the same LORD before whom the whole earth must still tremble, and the rock that gave His people water in the wilderness points forward to Christ, the true Rock, who leads His people through a greater exodus from death into life and offers living water to everyone who thirsts.

quiz.q: According to verse 2, what does Judah become when Israel departs from Egypt?

options.text: God's sanctuary, with Israel as His dominion.
options.note: The exodus was not merely political freedom but the creation of a people among whom God personally dwells.

options.text: An independent nation with no connection to God.
options.note: The verse explicitly names Judah as God's sanctuary, a place of His presence, not mere political independence.

options.text: A vassal state under Egyptian rule still.
options.note: The psalm celebrates departure from Egypt, the opposite of remaining a vassal state.

options.text: A temporary military outpost.
options.note: The term used, sanctuary, describes God's dwelling place, not merely a military installation.

quiz.q: What two bodies of water are personified as fleeing and turning back in verse 3?

options.text: The sea and the Jordan.
options.note: These recall the Red Sea crossing at the exodus and the Jordan crossing under Joshua.

options.text: The Nile and the Euphrates.
options.note: The text specifically names the sea and the Jordan, not the Nile or Euphrates.

options.text: The Dead Sea and the Sea of Galilee.
options.note: These particular bodies of water are not the ones named in the psalm's exodus imagery.

options.text: Only the Jordan, with no mention of the sea.
options.note: Both the sea and the Jordan are explicitly named together in verse 3.

quiz.q: What does the psalmist call all the earth to do in verse 7?

options.text: Tremble at the presence of the Lord, the God of Jacob.
options.note: This direct address extends the exodus narrative's implications to a universal summons to reverence.

options.text: Ignore what happened at the exodus as ancient history.
options.note: The verse commands active, reverent trembling, not indifference to God's past acts.

options.text: Rebuild the temple in Jerusalem immediately.
options.note: The command given is to tremble in reverence, not a specific building project.

options.text: Celebrate only once a year during Passover.
options.note: The command to tremble is not restricted to a single annual observance but is a standing summons.

quiz.q: What miraculous provision does verse 8 describe God performing?

options.text: Turning the rock into a pool, the flint into a fountain of water.
options.note: This recalls God's wilderness provision of water from the hardest stone, Exodus 17:6, Numbers 20:11.

options.text: Turning water into wine at a wedding feast.
options.note: That miracle belongs to Jesus' ministry in John 2, not this psalm's exodus and wilderness imagery.

options.text: Multiplying loaves and fishes for a crowd.
options.note: That is a Gospel miracle, not the wilderness rock imagery described in this psalm.

options.text: Causing manna to stop falling from heaven.
options.note: The psalm describes water flowing from rock, not the cessation of manna.

quiz.q: How does Paul apply the rock imagery of verse 8 in 1 Corinthians 10:4?

options.text: He identifies the rock that gave Israel water as a type of Christ Himself.
options.note: Paul explicitly states that Rock was Christ, connecting this psalm's imagery directly to Jesus as the source of living water.

options.text: He denies any connection between the rock and Christ.
options.note: Paul makes the connection explicit and direct, not a denial.

options.text: He applies it only to future events at the final judgment.
options.note: Paul's application concerns Israel's actual wilderness experience as a type fulfilled already in Christ, not solely a future event.

options.text: He uses it to argue for a different, non-messianic reading of the exodus.
options.note: Paul's reading is explicitly messianic, identifying the rock directly with Christ.

questions: This psalm recalls God's overwhelming presence at the exodus, so overwhelming that sea and mountains seemed to move. Where in your own life have you sensed God's presence powerfully enough to change how you saw a difficult circumstance?
The psalm calls the whole earth to tremble at God's presence. Is your own posture toward God more casual familiarity or reverent awe right now, and what would help restore appropriate trembling?

God turned the hardest flint into a flowing fountain for His thirsty people. What situation in your life currently feels as unyielding as flint, and how does this psalm encourage you to trust God's power to provide from it?
Paul identifies the wilderness rock as a type of Christ, the source of living water. Where are you currently seeking to satisfy a spiritual thirst with something other than Christ, the true Rock?
This psalm retells old history to shape present reverence and trust. What has God done in your past that you need to retell to yourself today to strengthen your faith for a current challenge?

translationNotes.note: Sanctuary renders miqdash, God's holy dwelling place, indicating that the exodus created not merely a free nation but a people among whom God Himself would personally dwell.

The sea, the Jordan, the mountains, and the hills are personified as fleeing, turning back, and skipping, vivid Hebrew poetic devices recalling the Red Sea and Jordan crossings as responses to God's overwhelming presence.
Flint renders challamish, an especially hard and unyielding stone, emphasizing that God's provision of water came from the most improbable and resistant of sources.
